title: Barley sugar
subtitle: Amber boiled sweets with a clean, faintly anise finish
excerpt: Sugar syrup cooked to hard-crack stage, then poured flat and twisted into amber sticks as it cools. An old British confection documented since the seventeenth century, now made with plain sugar syrup rather than the original barley water.
sourceNotes: Barley sugar is among the oldest documented British boiled sweets. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, gives the recipe under confectionery using a decoction of pearl barley for flavour. Earlier records from the seventeenth century describe barley water as the base. The lemon-flavoured plain-syrup version is the modern standard and is identical in method.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in a dry airtight tin lined with baking paper for 2 weeks. Humidity dissolves boiled sweets quickly.

content.text: Barley sugar is a plain boiled sweet cooked to hard-crack stage and shaped while still pliable. The amber colour develops naturally as the syrup reaches temperature: the sugar begins to caramelise at the top of the hard-crack range, giving the sweets their characteristic pale gold to amber hue. The twist is applied while the sugar is still warm and soft enough to move; it sets rigid as it cools. Timing and temperature are everything.
Sugar syrup at 150°C causes serious burns on contact. Keep children away from the stove. Have a bowl of cold water nearby. If hot syrup contacts skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes.
Work with oiled scissors to cut the strips: plain scissors drag and pull. Lightly oil the baking paper and your hands before handling the hot sugar.

Lightly oil a baking tray and line it with baking paper. Put the {{granulated-sugar}}, {{cream-of-tartar}}, {{lemon-juice}}, and 150 ml of water in a medium saucepan. Stir over a low heat until the sugar dissolves, then stop stirring. Bring to a boil over medium-high heat and cook until the thermometer reads 149-154°C (hard-crack stage). The syrup will be pale amber. If it colours unevenly, swirl the pan once.

Pour the syrup onto the oiled tray and leave for 2-3 minutes until it has cooled enough to handle at the edges but is still pliable. Using an oiled palette knife, fold the edges toward the centre. When the sugar has cooled to a workable mass (it should flex but not flow), cut into strips about 1 cm wide with oiled scissors and twist each strip into a spiral. Work quickly: the sugar hardens fast. Place the twisted sticks on fresh baking paper and leave to cool completely.
